doc: $~यो तपाईंले छानेको frequency मा अगाडि tick हुन्छ।
$~/tick tick tick/
$~प्रत्येक पटक त्यसो हुँदा, @Program नयाँ time value सँग फेरि मूल्याङ्कन हुन्छ।
$~उदाहरणका लागि:
$~\Time()\
$~यदि तपाईंले time लाई @Time.frequency दिनुभयो भने, यो त्यही दरमा tick हुन्छ। उदाहरणका लागि:
$~तर यो कति सानो हुन सक्छ भन्ने सीमा छ, किनकि अर्को tick मा प्रतिक्रिया दिनुअघि @Program लाई मूल्याङ्कन हुन समय चाहिन्छ।
$~सबैभन्दा सानो मान सम्भवतः करिब \20ms\ हुन्छ।

frequency.doc: $~time कति frequency मा tick हुनुपर्छ भन्ने मान। पूर्वनिर्धारित \33ms\ हो, करिब प्रति सेकेन्ड ३० पटक।

relative.doc: $~\⊤\ भएमा program पहिलो पटक मूल्याङ्कन हुँदा time 0 बाट सुरु हुन्छ। नभए, आजको UTC सुरु भएदेखि बितेका milliseconds बाट सुरु हुन्छ, जसले दिनको समय track गर्न दिन्छ।

doc: $~यो microphone को loudness को stream हो, 0 देखि 1 सम्म।
$~/bzzzzzzz/
$~यो तपाईंले छानेको frequency मा sample गरिन्छ। पूर्वनिर्धारित रूपमा frequency \30hz\, अर्थात् प्रति सेकेन्ड ३० पटक हुन्छ, तर तपाईं यसलाई कम frequent बनाउन सक्नुहुन्छ।
$~\Volume()\
$~दर्शक कति ठूलो आवाजमा छन् भनेर सुन्न यो निकै उपयोगी छ!
$~यो microphone को pitch को stream हो, hertz मा।
$~/hummmmm/
$~यो तपाईंले छानेको frequency मा sample गरिन्छ। मानव आवाज 20Hz देखि 5000Hz बीच हुन्छ, त्यसैले त्यही range का सङ्ख्याहरू सोच्नुहोस्।
$~\Pitch()\
$~कसैले बोल्दा वा गाउँदा आएको tone सुन्न यो उपयोगी छ।
$~यसले speech चिनेर त्यसलाई @Text मा बदल्छ।
$~/hello there!/
$~यसले तपाईंले भनेको कुरा सुनेर शब्दमा बदल्न browser को speech recognition प्रयोग गर्छ।
$~\Speech()\
$~भाषा बोल्न सक्ने तर पढ्न वा लेख्न अझै सिक्दै गरेका मानिसहरूका लागि यो उत्तम छ।
$~कुनै विशेष भाषाको speech चिन्न तपाईं language code दिन सक्नुहुन्छ:
$~\Speech(language: 'es-MX')\
$~भाषा नदिइएमा, यो तपाईंको preferred language मा default हुन्छ।
$~Speech recognition का लागि microphone access र Internet connection चाहिन्छ। usage limits पनि हुन सक्छन्, त्यसैले धेरै लामो समयसम्म खुला नराख्नुहोस्।

reset.doc: $~यो मान बदलिँदा जम्मा भएको speech खाली गरेर नयाँ सुरु गर्नुहोस्।

language.doc: $~BCP 47 language code, जस्तै 'en-US'। तपाईंको browser को speech recognition ले support नगरेमा error आउँछ। default तपाईंको preferred language हुन्छ।

limit.doc: $~जम्मा speech मा राखिने अधिकतम शब्द संख्या। सेट गरेपछि हालका शब्दहरू मात्र राखिन्छन् र पुराना हटाइन्छन्। नदिइएमा सबै शब्द अनिश्चित समयसम्म राखिन्छन्।

doc: $~यसले तपाईंको camera बाट प्रत्येक frame का लागि @Color को @List दिन्छ।
$~/bzzzzzzz/
$~त्यो @List मूलतः एउटा image जनाउँछ, तर त्यससँग के गर्ने भन्ने तपाईंमा निर्भर हुन्छ।
$~तपाईं धेरै @Phrase प्रयोग गरेर image देखाउने प्रयास गर्न सक्नुहुन्छ, जुन निकै राम्रो देखिन सक्छ। यो copy गरेर प्रयास गर्नुहोस्...
$~वा तपाईं colors विश्लेषण गरेर light on/off छ कि छैन, वा कुनै particular color common छ कि छैन निर्णय गर्न सक्नुहुन्छ, जसले दर्शकले देखाएका colors बाट performance प्रभावित गराउँछ।

width.doc: $~एउटा row मा sample गर्ने @Color को संख्या।

height.doc: $~एउटा column मा sample गर्ने @Color को संख्या।

doc: $~यसले camera ले देखेको हात track गर्छ: हात कहाँ छ, कुन fingers माथि छन्, र कुन दिशातिर फर्किएको छ।
$~/wiggle wiggle/
$~हात माथि उठाउनुहोस्, हल्लाउनुहोस्, औँलामा गन्नुहोस्; @Hand ले पछ्याउँछ।

$~@Hand ले भित्रबाट machine-learning model प्रयोग गर्छ। program मा पहिलो पटक प्रयोग गर्दा model download हुन केही सेकेन्ड लाग्छ, र load हुँदै गर्दा stage को माथिल्लो-दायाँ कुनामा सानो spinning hand देखिन्छ। त्यसपछि यो cached रहन्छ र तुरुन्त सुरु हुन्छ।
$~प्रत्येक frame मा @Hand ले @Gesture दिन्छ। inputs को पूरा सूची र तिनको अर्थका लागि त्यहाँ हेर्नुहोस्।
$~यो प्रयास गर्नुहोस्; यसले तपाईंको वास्तविक हात जहाँ छ त्यहाँ हातको pose सँग मिल्ने emoji देखाउँछ:

$~\hand: Hand()
Phrase((hand.open ? '🖐️' '✊') place: hand.place)\

resolution.doc: $~hand model चलाउनुअघि camera sample गर्ने pixel चौडाइ। ठूलो मानले landmarks अलि बढी सही दिन सक्छ, तर प्रत्येक frame मा बढी समय लाग्छ।

doc: $~यसले @Phrase वा @Group को @List एक पटकमा एक-एक गरी देखाउँछ, अन्तिममा रोकिँदै।
$~/Boop boop boop/

$~timing नियन्त्रण गर्न, प्रत्येक output मा @Phrase.duration सेट गर्न सक्नुहुन्छ; त्यसले अर्कोमा जानुअघि त्यति समय देखाउँछ, र तपाईंले राखेका @Phrase.entering वा @Phrase.exiting transitions प्रयोग गर्छ। ती @Sequence भएमा त्यसकै duration प्रयोग हुन्छ।
$~यी सबैले तलको सानो @Scene जस्ता काम गर्न दिन्छ, जसले यी तीन phrases क्रमशः देखाउँछ:

$~\Scene([
	Phrase('Hello' duration: 1s)
	Phrase('How are you?' duration: 2s rotation: 5° entering: Pose(rotation: 0°))
	Phrase('I am fine')
])\
$~पहिलो output एक सेकेन्ड, त्यसपछि अर्को दुई सेकेन्ड, पाँच degree rotation सहित animated भएर, अनि अन्तिम देखिएको याद गर्नुभयो?
$~@Scene बाट output र animations का धेरै विस्तृत sequences बनाउन सक्नुहुन्छ, र अरू @Phrase वा @Group जस्तै तिनका केही भाग dynamic वा interactive बनाउन सक्नुहुन्छ।
$~यदि nested animated output छ, जस्तै animated @Phrase भएको @Group, @Scene ले @Group भित्रको सबै animated content सकिन पर्खन्छ।
$~अनि एउटा सानो रहस्य! तपाईं list मा @Boolean पनि राख्न सक्नुहुन्छ, र यदि ती \⊥\ छन् भने @Scene pause भएर true हुन पर्खन्छ।
$~उदाहरणका लागि, @Phrase को sequence बनाएर @Button बदलिँदा मात्र अगाडि बढाउन चाहनुभयो भने, यसो गर्न सक्नुहुन्छ:

$~\click: ∆ Button()
Scene([
	Phrase('Hello')
	click
	Phrase('How are you?' duration: 0.25s rotation: 5° entering: Pose(rotation: 0°))
	click
	Phrase('I am fine')
])\
$~प्रत्येक @Phrase पछि pause भएर अगाडि बढ्नुअघि @Button बदलिन पर्खेको देख्नुभयो?
$~यसले gravity र physics प्रयोग गरेर सुरुआती @Phrase सार्छ।
$~/boing boing boing/
$~तपाईंले यसलाई सुरुआती @Phrase दिनुहुन्छ, त्यसपछि यसले gravity का आधारमा नयाँ place र rotation सहित त्यसलाई सुधार्छ।
$~यसले bouncing emoji बनाउने जस्ता सरल काम गर्न दिन्छ:
$~\Motion(Phrase('o') startplace: Place(0m 10m))\
$~o उफ्रिएको देख्नुभयो? पहिलो evaluation मा तपाईंले यसलाई @Stage मा माथिल्लो place दिनुहुन्छ, तर त्यसपछि यसले @None पाउँछ, जसले @Motion लाई gravity ले राख्ने position मा बदल्न दिन्छ।
$~यसलाई configure गर्ने अरू धेरै तरिका तल हेर्नुहोस्।
$~यो तपाईंका दर्शकले type गर्ने @Text messages को stream हो।
$~उदाहरणका लागि, यो सरल program ले message 'hello' हो कि होइन जाँच गर्छ; हो भने program 'hi' मा, नभए 'huh' मा मूल्याङ्कन हुन्छ।
$~\Chat().has('hello') ? 'hi!' 'huh?'\
$~यत्ति हो! यसबाट chat bots, text adventures, वा अरू performances का लागि text-based control schemes जस्ता सबै किसिमका performances बनाउन सक्नुहुन्छ।
$~यसले प्रत्येक अक्षरको glyph outline लाई @Place को @List का रूपमा trace गर्छ।
$~यसलाई text र font face दिनुहोस्, यसले प्रत्येक glyph को outline point by point trace गर्छ।
$~\Contour('a' "Noto Sans")\
$~ती @Place मानहरू @Phrase को @List लाई दिनुहोस्, outline dots का रूपमा कोरिएको देख्न:
$~\Stage(Contour('a' "Noto Sans" 10m).translate(ƒ(point) Phrase('•' place: point)))\

$~तपाईं outline लाई meters को @Number ले scale गर्न, origin लाई @Place ले सार्न, font weight छान्न, italics खोल्न, र points बीचको `spacing` meters मा सेट गर्न सक्नुहुन्छ। सानो `spacing` ले lines र curves दुवैमा evenly spaced points सहित outline अझ नजिकबाट trace गर्छ।
$~केही glyphs, जस्तै color emoji, को outline हुँदैन, त्यसैले points थपिँदैन। font fetch हुँदै गर्दा यसले खाली @List दिन्छ, अनि तयार भएपछि भर्छ।

inputs.doc: $~जसका glyph outlines trace गर्नुपर्ने text।
$~glyphs trace गर्ने font face।
$~outline scale गर्ने glyph को उचाइ, meters मा।
$~हरेक point offset गर्ने optional origin @Place।
$~प्रयोग गर्ने font weight, face ले support गर्ने नजिकको मानमा मिलाइन्छ।
$~face सँग italic version भए त्यसलाई प्रयोग गर्ने कि नगर्ने।
$~outline का sampled points बीचको दूरी meters मा, lines र curves दुवैमा consistent राखिन्छ। सानो मानले अझ fine trace गर्छ, न्यूनतम 0.01 meters सम्म।
$~points को क्रम: 'forward' वा 'backward'।

error.connection: $~font ल्याउन internet पुग्न सकिनँ, त्यसैले अक्षरहरू trace गर्न सक्दिनँ।
error.unavailable: $~त्यो font को file भेटिएन, त्यसैले अक्षरहरू trace गर्न सक्दिनँ।
error.unreadable: $~font download भयो, तर पढ्न सकिनँ, त्यसैले अक्षरहरू trace गर्न सक्दिनँ।
error.outline: $~font load भयो, तर यी अक्षरहरूको shape पाउन सकिनँ।

doc: $~यसले तपाईंका दर्शकलाई @Output लाई @Stage को @Place मा drag गर्न दिन्छ।
$~@Stage मा @Output राख्ने धेरै तरिका छन्: स्पष्ट @Place दिनुहोस्, @Motion प्रयोग गरेर gravity लाई काम गर्न दिनुहोस्, वा मिलाउनका लागि @Group मा राख्नुहोस्।
$~तर कहिलेकाहीँ @Output @Stage मा कहाँ जाने भन्ने control /दर्शक/ लाई दिन चाहनुहुन्छ।
$~यसरी काम गर्छ: @Placement बनाउनुहोस् र @Output को @Place लाई दिनुहोस्:
$~\Phrase('hi' place: Placement())\
$~त्यसपछि, दर्शकले arrow key प्रयोग गर्दा वा stage मा click/tap गर्दा, @Placement ले चाहेको दिशामा सर्ने नयाँ @Place बनाउँछ।
$~यसलाई आफ्नो program मा copy गरेर pointer वा keyboard बाट @Output सारेर हेर्नुहोस्।
$~तपाईं @Placement customize गर्न सक्नुहुन्छ: केही dimensions मा movement खोल्ने/बन्द गर्ने, @Place कति टाढा सर्छ बदल्ने, र stream सुरु हुने initial @Place सेट गर्ने।

inputs.doc: $~सुरु गर्ने initial place।
$~दर्शकले move माग्दा place कति meters सर्नुपर्छ।
$~true भएमा horizontal axis मा movement दिन्छ। default मा on हुन्छ।
$~true भएमा vertical axis मा movement दिन्छ। default मा on हुन्छ।
$~true भएमा + र - keys बाट z-axis मा movement दिन्छ।

doc: $~यसले तपाईंले दिएको web address बाट text fetch गर्छ।
$~Internet रोचक छ, आपसमा documents साझा गर्ने connected computers को पूरै संसार।
$~यो stream त्यसमा connect हुन्छ; यसलाई यसरी URL दिनुहोस्:
$~\Webpage('https://wordplay.dev')\

$~अनि <CSS@https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/CSS/First_steps/What_is_CSS> भन्ने कुरा पनि छ, जसले web page मा चीजहरू query गर्न दिन्छ। CSS selection query दिनुहोस्, यसले त्यो query सँग मिल्ने text मात्र ल्याउँछ। यो उदाहरणले level one headers ल्याउँछ।
$~\Webpage('https://wordplay.dev' 'h1')\

$~यसमा धेरै कुरा बिग्रन सक्छन्। internet connection हराएमा, URL केहीमा resolve नभएमा, URL public नभएमा, वा URL HTML page नभएमा exception आउन सक्छ। काम गर्ने page भेटिएमा, percent complete जनाउने केही @Number र त्यसपछि page का words को @List पाउनुहुनेछ।

frequency.doc: $~page फेरि fetch हुनुअघि बित्नुपर्ने minutes को संख्या।

doc: $~named @Output हरू एक-अर्कासँग ठोक्किँदा आउने @Rebound मानहरूको stream हो।
$~@Output हरू ठोक्किँदा normal bounce बाहेक केही विशेष गर्न यो राम्रो तरिका हो।
$~@Output को नाम दिनुहोस्, त्यो नाम अर्कोमा ठोक्किँदा यसले नयाँ @Rebound मान बनाउँछ। @Rebound मा ठोक्किएका नामहरू र collision direction बारे जानकारी हुन्छ।
$~र यदि दुई नाम दिनुभयो भने, ती दुई नाम एक-अर्कासँग ठोक्किँदा मात्र नयाँ मान बनाउँछ।
$~नयाँ मान बनाएको तुरुन्तपछि यसले \ø\ बनाउँछ, किनकि collision भएपछि सकिन्छ। यसले अब collision छैन भन्ने जनाउँछ।

Rebound.doc: $~म @Collision बाट आउँछु र कोसँग ठोक्कियो तथा collision कुन दिशामा भयो भन्ने जनाउँछु। normal physics बाहेक collision मा विशेष प्रतिक्रिया दिने कि नदिने निर्णय गर्न मलाई प्रयोग गर्नुहोस्।

direction.doc: $~collision को subject को सापेक्ष collision को direction र magnitude।

subject.doc: $~subject ले hit गरेको output को नाम।

object.doc: $~subject लाई hit गर्ने output को नाम।

Direction.doc: $~म x र y axes मा direction र magnitude हुँ।

x.doc: $~x-axis मा direction को direction र magnitude।

y.doc: $~y-axis मा direction को direction र magnitude।

doc: $?I am not a @StructureDefinition you can actually make. But I am a very important one, as I inspire the most important elements of our dance: @Phrase, @Group, and @Stage.
$?Go meet them to learn more about how to use them.
$?I arrange @Phrase and @Group together on @Stage with an @Arrangement. Oh hello, how are you? I'm always fine when others are around, so it's great to be with you!
$?To work, I need you to give me an @Arrangement, and then a @List of @Output to arrange.
$?For example, here I am with a @Stack arrangement and a few @Phrase to stack vertically:
$?\Group(Stack() [Phrase('first') Phrase('second')])\
$?How exactly I arrange things depend on the @Arrangement you give me.

layout.doc: $?The arrangement to use to put @Output in their places.

matter.doc: $?How I should react if I were to bump into something else with matter.

size.doc: $?How tall the wonderful content inside me should be, unless they have a size of their own!

face.doc: $?The name of the font face content inside me should have, unless they have their own face to use.

place.doc: $?The place on stage where I should be. The content inside me will be arranged relative to there.

doc: $?The same as @Phrase.name!
$?A description to use for audience members who can't see visual output.

color.doc: $?The @Color that content inside me should be, unless they have their own color.

opacity.doc: $?How transparent everything inside me should be, between \0\ and \1\, unless overridden by a different @Pose.

offset.doc: $?A @Place indicating how offset from my normal @Place it should be, unless overridden by a different @Pose. Helpful for wiggling in place.

rotation.doc: $?How tilted should I be around my center, my @Pose has a different one.

scale.doc: $?How big I should be relative to my original size.

doc: $?I represent the loveliest of @Text on @Stage. Hello, hello! Remember me? How could anyone forget /me/.
$?Just make me like this, and I'll appear on @Stage:
$?\Phrase('magnificent!')\
$?I need @TextLiteral or @FormattedLiteral to show. With @FormattedLiteral, I can use things like \`*bold* and /italic/`\ or refer to custom @ConceptLink you create.
$?You can also optionally give me everything an @Output can do, including changing my size, font, rotation, and do all of my incredible dances with @Pose and @Sequence.
$?You can also select me on @Stage and edit me with the palette 🎨 next door — it's a great way to try out different fonts and pick the right /face/ for your @Text.

text.doc: $?The characters to show on @Stage, a @TextLiteral or @FormattedLiteral.

size.doc: $?How tall I should be, in meters!

face.doc: $?The name of the font face I should put on. The easiest way to choose one is to select me and open the palette 🎨 next door, where you can preview fonts and pick a /face/ without typing its name.

place.doc: $?The place on stage where I should be.

wrap.doc: $?The edge at which I should wrap symbols or \ø\ if I shouldn't wrap them.

alignment.doc: $?If there's a @Phrase.wrap boundary set, whether I should align symbols to the start, center, or end of edge.

direction.doc: $?Whether symbols are written horizontally or vertically, and if vertical and @Phrase.wrap is set, whether text wraps left or right.

matter.doc: $?The properties to use if I bump into things!

aura.doc: $?The optional @Aura to show behind me. Make me glow!

doc: $?A name you give me! This is helpful for many things.
$?First, if I have a name, I'll use it to describe myself in screen reader descriptions.
$?Second, when animating, you might have multiple different expressions that are supposed to represent the same content on stage; give them the same name and they will animate as one.
$?Finally, I'm helpful with @Choice: the names you give me appear in that stream.
$?You can give me many different names, each in a different language, if it's helpful. I'll always use the name in the first selected language.
$?A custom description to use for audience members who can't see visual output.

selectable.doc: $?If \⊤\, indicates that I can be selected via a pointer or keyboard.

color.doc: $?The @Color I should be by default, unless overridden by a different @Pose.

opacity.doc: $?How transparent I should be by default, between \0\ and \1\, unless overridden by a different @Pose. Helpful for fading in and out.

offset.doc: $?A @Place indicating how offset from my @Place it should be, unless overridden by a different @Pose. Helpful for wiggling in place.

rotation.doc: $?The amount in degrees I should should be rotated around its center, unless overridden by a different @Pose.

scale.doc: $?How magnified I should be relative to its original size, unless overridden by a different @Pose.

flipx.doc: $?Whether I should be mirrored on the x-axis, unless overridden by a different @Pose.

flipy.doc: $?Whether I should be mirrored on the y-axis, unless overridden by a different @Pose.

entering.doc: $?The @Pose or @Sequence I should do upon entering the stage.

resting.doc: $?The @Pose or @Sequence I should do after I've entered stage, before I exit, and while my @Place isn't changing. If you don't give me one, I'll just use my defaults.

moving.doc: $?The @Pose or @Sequence I should do when moving places on stage, instead of my defaults.

exiting.doc: $?The @Pose or @Sequence to should do before exiting stage.

duration.doc: $?The duration to apply when moving to a different place on stage.

style.doc: $?The animation style to use when moving to a different place on stage.

Arrangement.doc: $?I am an inspiration to the many other kinds of arrangement in the Verse, including @Row, @Stack, @Grid, and @Free. I work closely with @Group to

Row.doc: $?I am @Row, a horizontal @Arrangement of @Output, with optional padding in between. Have you met my twin, @Stack?

alignment.doc: $?Whether to align text at the start, center, or end on each column.

padding.doc: $?The amount of padding to place between output.

Stack.doc: $?I am @Stack, a vertical @Arrangement of @Output, with optional padding in between. Have you met my twin, @Row?

alignment.doc: $?Whether to align text at the start, center, or end on each row.

padding.doc: $?The amount of padding to place between output.

Grid.doc: $?I am grid of @Output. Give me a row and column count and I'll make a tidy arrangement with optional padding and cell sizes.

rows.doc: $?How many rows to make in the grid.

columns.doc: $?How many columns to make in the grid.

padding.doc: $?How much padding to place between cells.

doc: $?I'm an @Arrangement that lets each @Output sit wherever its @Place says. I'm like, whatever. Sit wherever you want! Just sit somewhere! Make sure all the @Output you give me have a @Place, otherwise they won't know where to go.
$?Oh, and remember that the @Place you give each @Output is relative to the @Group's @Place! So if you're wondering why things aren't appearing where you expect, try giving the @Group a place too.

Shape.doc: $?I'm an inspiration to all shapes. I'm useful for telling @Stage what shape to be.

form.doc: $?I'm the kind of shape to show. Each shape requires different information to define its arrangement.

name.doc: $?I'm the name you can use, for animations and @Collision. For example, if a represent the ground, you might want to call me 'ground'.

doc: $?A description to use for audience members who can't see visual output.

selectable.doc: $?Whether I can be selected as part of @Choice.

offset.doc: $?How far from my place I should appear, while remaining in place.

rotation.doc: $?How rotated I should be. This affects @Collision.

scale.doc: $?How magnified I should be, without changing my actual size.

flipx.doc: $?Whether to mirror me on my x-axis.

flipy.doc: $?Whether to mirror me on my y-axis.

entering.doc: $?The @Pose or @Sequence I should do when entering @Stage.

resting.doc: $?The @Pose or @Sequence I should do after entering and while I'm not moving.

moving.doc: $?The @Pose or @Sequence I should do when moving places.

exiting.doc: $?The @Pose or @Sequence I should do when leaving @Stage.

duration.doc: $?How long my animations should take if they are a single @Pose.

doc: $?I speak text aloud instead of showing it on @Stage. Hi!
$?Just give me something to say, and I'll say it every time your program evaluates:
$?\Say('blah blah blah')\
$?I'll use the @Language of the @TextLiteral if it has one, otherwise I'll use the selected language.
$?You can select a voice in settings.

Form.doc: $?I am an abstract form, like a @Rectangle or @Circle.

Rectangle.doc: $?I am a rectangle, useful for making @Stage have a boundary the size of your choosing.

left.doc: $?The left edge of the stage on the x-axis

top.doc: $?The top edge of the stage on the y-axes

right.doc: $?The right edge of the stage on the x-axis

bottom.doc: $?The bottom edge of the stage on the y-axis

Circle.doc: $?I am a circle, useful for making shapes on @Stage.

Polygon.doc: $?I am a 'regular' polygon with equal length sides and angles, useful for making shapes on @Stage.

sides.doc: $?The number of sides of the polygon

doc: $?I capture a @Pose for @Output to hold, and I'm the building block of their movements. You know when someone strikes the most amazing way of standing, pauses, and everyone looks? That's me.
$?So much goes into a pose. Check out my many inputs to see what kinds of poses you might make!

style.doc: $?The style of animation to use when moving to this pose.

color.doc: $?The @Color a @Output should be in this pose, instead of it's default.

opacity.doc: $?How transparent a @Output should be, between \0\ and \1\, instead of it's default. Helpful for fading in and out.

offset.doc: $?A @Place indicating how offset from a @Output's place it should be, instead of it's default. Helpful for wiggling in place.

rotation.doc: $?How rotated a @Output should be, instead of it's default.

scale.doc: $?How magnified a @Output should be relative to its original size, instead of it's default.

flipx.doc: $?Whether a @Output should be mirrored on the x-axis, instead of its default.

flipy.doc: $?Whether a @Output should be mirrored on the y-axis, instead of it's default.

doc: $?I am a visible color, made of three essential dimensions.
$?Here are some common colors around the color wheel, at medium brightness and high chroma:
$?For the eleven most common color names, you don't have to make me from scratch. I have shortcuts on my name:
$?\Color.red\
$?\Color.blue\

$?These shortcuts work in your language too, so a French speaker can write \couleur.rouge\ and a Japanese speaker can write \色.赤\. The shortcuts are: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, purple, brown, pink, black, white, and gray.

lightness.doc: $?How light I should be from \0\ to \1\, from black at \0\, to grey at \0.5\, to white at \1\.

chroma.doc: $?How much color I should have, from \0\ to \∞\. No color means grey, higher numbers mean the more color.

hue.doc: $?What color I should be, on a color wheel, from magenta \0\, red \30\, green \120\, to blue \270\.

doc: $?Give a random color.
$?With no colors, I pick one of the basic colors at random:
$?\Color.random()\
$?With one color, I keep its lightness and chroma but pick a random hue:
$?\Color.random(Color.blue)\
$?With two colors, I pick a random lightness, chroma, and hue between the two:
$?\Color.random(Color.black Color.white)\

inputs.doc: $?An optional color. Alone, I keep its lightness and chroma and randomize the hue. With a second color, it's one end of the range to pick from.
$?An optional second color. When given, I pick a random lightness, chroma, and hue between the two colors.

doc: $?Give a lighter version of me, raising my lightness by a percent (5% by default).
$?\Color.blue.lighter()\
$?\Color.blue.lighter(20%)\

inputs.doc: $?How much to raise the lightness, as a percent. Defaults to 5%.

doc: $?Give a darker version of me, lowering my lightness by a percent (5% by default).
$?\Color.blue.darker()\
$?\Color.blue.darker(20%)\

inputs.doc: $?How much to lower the lightness, as a percent. Defaults to 5%.

doc: $?I animate @Output through a series of @Pose over time! Do you want to dance with me? It's easy.
$?You just need to give me a @Map, where each key represents what percent along we are in the dance, and each value of those keys is a @Pose to be.
$?There are /so/ many different ways you can animate with this! For example, here's a simple one:
$?\Phrase('hi' resting:Sequence({0%: Pose(rotation: 360°) 100%: Pose(rotation: 0°)})\
$?This says, /at the beginning (0%), start at tilt 360, and end at tilt 0/. That'll spin us around in circles forever, since I'm set as the @Phrase's rest pose!
$?Try your own creative dances by playing with other inputs.

poses.doc: $?A @Map of percentages between 0% and 100%, each paired with a @Pose. You don't have to provide all the percents; I will smoothly move a @Output between the ones you give me.

duration.doc: $?How long should I do this dance? If I'm to repeat it, I won't add any time to the duration, I'll just dance faster.

style.doc: $?The style should I use to for the dance.

count.doc: $?How many times the sequence should repeat before it is done. This is really helpful for when I enter stage, move on stage, or exit on stage, but when for a rest sequence, it's ignored, since I can rest forever.

description.doc: $?An optional description of this sequence for viewers who can't see the animation.

Place.doc: $?I'm a location on @Stage. All my inputs are optional, because I'm at the center by default.

Gesture.doc: $?I'm a snapshot of a hand pose detected by the camera, emitted by the @Hand stream. My place tells you where the hand is on stage, my fingers tells you how many are extended, my open is true when the hand is open (3+ fingers extended), and my thumb, index, middle, ring, and pinky each say which specific fingers are extended. My palm is true when the palm faces the camera, false when the back of the hand does.

open.doc: $?@True if the hand is open (3+ fingers extended), @False if it's a fist.

fingers.doc: $?How many fingers are extended, from 0 (fist) to 5 (all fingers up, including the thumb).

index.doc: $?@True if the index finger is extended.

middle.doc: $?@True if the middle finger is extended.

ring.doc: $?@True if the ring finger is extended.

pinky.doc: $?@True if the pinky finger is extended.

palm.doc: $?@True if the palm faces the camera, @False if the back of the hand does.

Velocity.doc: $?I'm how fast @Output moves on @Stage — meters per second along x and y, and degrees per second of rotation. My inputs are optional; by default nothing moves.

x.doc: $?How many meters to move each second on the x-axis.

y.doc: $?How many meters to move each second on the y-axis.

angle.doc: $?How many degrees to rotate each second

Result.doc: $?I'm one match from a pattern search with \⌕\: the matched text, where it is, and any captures.

groups.doc: $?Each capture's name to the text it matched.

starts.doc: $?Each capture's name to where it starts.

ends.doc: $?Each capture's name to where it ends.

Matter.doc: $?I'm physical properties of output, which influence how I interact with other output on stage.

bounciness.doc: $?How much of my energy to keep on collision, 0 means none, 1 means all of it.

friction.doc: $?How much to keep sliding; 0 means none, 1 means forever.

roundedness.doc: $?How much to round the corners of the output; 0 means none and 1 means 100% of its size, making the sizes circular.

text.doc: $?Whether it can collide with other output.

shapes.doc: $?Whether it can collide with other shapes.

doc: $?I make @Phrase GLOW! I am an AURA. Like this:

$?\Phrase(
	'I am GLOWING!' 
	aura: Aura(Color(50% 100 118°) 0.1m 0m 0.1m
)\

blur.doc: $?How blurry the @Aura should be. \0m\ means not blurry at all.

offsetX.doc: $?How far left or right I should appear. \0m\ is directly under.

offsetY.doc: $?How far up or down I should appear.\0m\ is directly under.

doc: $?I SHOW YOUR @Output TO THE AUDIENCE. HI. STAGE HERE. TELL ME WHAT TO SHOW AND I WILL SHOW IT.
$?\Stage([Phrase('stufffffff')])\
$?IF YOU WANT, GIVE ME A BACKGROUND @Color AND I WILL LIGHT THE STAGE ACCORDINGLY.
$?\Stage([Phrase('stufffffff')] background: Color(75% 50 100°))\
$?YOU MAY ALSO GIVE ME A FRAME BORDER AND I WILL CROP.
$?\Stage([Phrase('stufffffff')] background: Color(75% 50 100°) frame: Rectangle(-1m -1m 1m 1m))\

content.doc: $?The list of @Output to show on stage.

frame.doc: $?The shape and size of frame to place around the stage, hiding everything outside it.

place.doc: $?IF I WERE A CAMERA, THIS IS WHERE I AM LOOKING

doc: $?SAME AS @Phrase.name!
$?A description to use for audience members who can't see visual output.

gravity.doc: $?The gravity to apply to output whose place is in @Motion.

sway.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that sways back and forth around a @Output's center.

angle.doc: $?How much to tilt in the sway.

bounce.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that bounces @Output a given height.

spin.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that rotates @Output around its center.

fadein.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that fades @Output in from invisible to visible.

fadeout.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that fades @Output from visible to invisible. Try me in an exiting @Sequence!

popup.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that makes @Output scale in quickly, then shrink to its normal size.

shake.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that makes it look like a @Output is scared.

pulse.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that gently scales @Output up and back down to draw attention.

amount.doc: $?How much to scale at the peak.

heartbeat.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that scales @Output with two quick beats, like a heartbeat.

amount.doc: $?How much to scale on each beat.

tada.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that makes @Output celebrate with a scale and wiggle.

amount.doc: $?How much to scale during the celebration.

wiggle.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that tilts and slides @Output side to side.

angle.doc: $?How much to tilt in the wiggle.

flash.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that blinks @Output on and off.

zoomin.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that scales @Output in from nothing to full size. Try me in an entering @Sequence!

fadeinup.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that fades @Output in while sliding it up into place. Try me in an entering @Sequence!

fadeindown.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that fades @Output in while sliding it down into place. Try me in an entering @Sequence!

fadeinleft.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that fades @Output in while sliding it into place from the left. Try me in an entering @Sequence!

fadeinright.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that fades @Output in while sliding it into place from the right. Try me in an entering @Sequence!

rotatein.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that spins @Output into place while fading it in. Try me in an entering @Sequence!

zoomout.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that scales @Output out to nothing. Try me in an exiting @Sequence!

fadeoutup.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that fades @Output out while sliding it up. Try me in an exiting @Sequence!

fadeoutdown.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that fades @Output out while sliding it down. Try me in an exiting @Sequence!

fadeoutleft.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that fades @Output out while sliding it left. Try me in an exiting @Sequence!

fadeoutright.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that fades @Output out while sliding it right. Try me in an exiting @Sequence!

rotateout.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that spins @Output away while fading it out. Try me in an exiting @Sequence!

rainbow.doc: $?I create a @Sequence that cycles @Output through the colors of the rainbow.

doc: $?You know how projects can have more than one @Source file? I let you create a @Source based on your project's logic. This is really helpful if you want to save some data between different evaluations of your project.
$?For example, imagine you wanted to make a simple counter that counts up by one each time you press a mouse button. You might use this to remember how many times you did something.

$?\↓ count
[
	Phrase(`\count\ times!`)
	Source('count' count … ∆ Button() … count + 1 )
]\

$?Try copying it, making a new @Source called /count/ and typing 0 in it, to start the count at 0. This little project will get the value in the /count/ source and each time the mouse button is pressed, edits the /count/ @Source with to be the current /count/ value plus /1/.

name.doc: $?The name of the source file to create or update.

value.doc: $?The data value that the source file should be created or updated with.

DynamicEditLimitException.explanation: $?This project saved data to @Source files too many times, too quickly. Make sure it only updates @Source in response to an input, and not too fast.

ReadOnlyEditException.explanation: $?This project remembers data, but you don't have rights to edit it. Copy it if you want to try it.

EmptySourceNameException.explanation: $?The @Source given had an empty name, so we couldn't save it.

ProjectSizeLimitException.explanation: $?This project has too much text, so we can't save it.

tour.editor: $?All your /code/ goes in the *editor*. Every Wordplay program *evaluates to a value*, and that value is what shows up on the *stage*.
tour.textBlocks: $?This toggle switches between two ways of editing: /text/ and /blocks/. *Text* is more flexible but easier to make mistakes in. *Blocks* are less error prone but slower and more rigid.
tour.toolbar: $?These /toolbar/ controls let you *select all*, *zoom in* and *out*, *undo*, *redo*, *cut*, *copy*, and *paste*.
tour.shortcuts: $?The /shortcuts/ button opens a list of *keyboard shortcuts* you can use to edit more quickly.

annotations.label: $?conflicts and help
annotations.cursor: $?This is *$node*$type[ and they are of type $type|]$description[. They $description.|]
annotations.space: $?This is space! Who knew nothing could say so much?

output.label: $?stage
output.multiselect: $?Use tab and the alt and arrow keys to move between outputs. Press space to add or remove the focused output from your selection. Press enter to select just that output and open the palette. Press escape to clear your selection. Press control or command and A to select all outputs.

tour.stage: $?The /stage/ shows your program's *current value*. That value might be a *raw value* like a number or list, *visual output* you can see, or even *sound* you can hear.
tour.reset: $?The /reset/ button rewinds your program's evaluation back to the *beginning*, replaying any time, key, or input streams from scratch.
tour.zoom: $?The /zoom/ buttons let you zoom *in* and *out* on the stage so you can see your output more clearly or in greater detail.
tour.grid: $?The /grid/ button shows a *grid* on the stage, helpful for placing things precisely while you design.
tour.lock: $?The /lock/ button locks the stage's view in place. When *locked*, the stage stays still; when *unlocked*, you can pan and zoom freely.
tour.animationSpeed: $?The /animation speed/ control sets how fast animations play. The same control is in *settings*, so you can change it from either place.

button.play: $?evaluate the program to the end, responding to inputs in real time
button.pause: $?pause the program, enabling stepping forward and backwards

tour.timeline: $?The /timeline/ lets you step *forward* and *backward* through your program's evaluation, one step at a time. It's a great way to see *how* your program computes its value, and to build a mental model of how Wordplay programs evaluate, especially if you're not seeing the behavior you want.
tour.reset: $?The /reset/ button rewinds your program's evaluation all the way back to the *beginning*, so you can start watching the evaluation from scratch.
tour.playMode: $?The program is now /playing/. In *play* mode, your program runs until it's done (if ever), responding to inputs in real time.
tour.pauseMode: $?The program is now /paused/. In *pause* mode, you can step *forward* and *backward* through evaluation. The /annotations/ window now shows what's happening at each step, and the *current value* of each part of your program appears next to it in the editor.
tour.annotations: $?The /annotations/ window is where explanations of each step appear. As you step through your program, this is where you'll read what just happened.
tour.editor: $?The /editor/ shows your code. While stepping, the *value* of each part of your program will appear next to that part *after* it's been evaluated, so you can watch values build up as the program runs.
tour.history: $?The /history/ shows every *input* your program has received, like keys, time ticks, and pointer events. It's *draggable*, so you can return to any moment in the program's evaluation history just by dragging.
tour.stepControls: $?The /step controls/ let you move through evaluation precisely. You can jump to the *start* or *end*, step *forward* or *backward* one step at a time, jump by *one input*, step *out* of a function, or jump to the *currently selected* part of the code.

content: $?Have you ever felt like coding was just for English-fluent, non-disabled Westerners who grew up with computers?
$?Yeah, us too.

$?This isn't an accident. Since the dawn of computing, programming languages have been designed and built by mostly the same group of people -- mostly white, cis, English-speaking men in academia and industry in the US and Europe, and a few incredible woman mathematicians. They did their work in a time of post-colonial, winner-take-all power, and programming languages were a key tool for securing that power.
$?This history has led to a vision of computation that is primarily about speed, logic, profit, and domination.

$?This is an injustice. Because computing, for better and worse, now undergirds daily life in visible and invisible ways, and the people who have access to create with it are the ones most like its creators. The rest of humanity remains beholden to this power, because imagining anything different requires literacy, gatekept by language barriers, accessibility barriers, economic barriers, and inequities in public education.

$?*Wordplay* aspires to help change this. It's a programming platform designed to be global, supporting /all/ of the world's languages, but also be /about/ the world's languages. A platform on which everyone can create, with whatever abilities they have, to share interactive content that anyone can experience. For youth and young adults who want to express themselves through interactive words, emojis, and typography, playfully and artfully. Not with the goal of getting power for themselves, but to create a computational world that recognize the incredible strength and necessity of our beautiful differences.

$?We're a community of designers, educators, and developers trying to bring this vision to life. We are people of color, we are trans, we are queer, we are disabled, we are immigrants, we are refugees. We're centered at the <University of Washington@https://washington.edu> <Information School@https://ischool.uw.edu/> in Seattle, Washington, USA, a place that aspires to welcome everyone and undo the ableist and racist ravages of colonialism that continue today. Making this programming language and platform is a small part of that mission, offering a glimpse of a future of computing where /everyone/ belongs.

$?We'd love your help. Learn <how to contribute@https://github.com/wordplaydev/wordplay/wiki/contribute>, <donate@://donate>, or track our <progress@https://github.com/amyjko/wordplay/milestones>. Write our community organizer <Amy@https://amyjko.phd> if you have questions.
$?These are the latest new features, improvements, and bug fixes in Wordplay. We release updates weekly on most Saturdays, unless we find an urgent bug.

prompt.login: $?Login to save your projects, collaborate, and share:
prompt.forgot: $?*Forgot your password?* Unfortunately, we cannot recover your account since we do not gather contact information.
prompt.enter: $?It looks like your login link came from a different browser or device. Can you enter your email again, just so we're sure it's you?
prompt.play: $?You're logged in, we can save your projects online now! Want to create something?
prompt.passwordrule: $?Passwords must be at least 6 characters long; if you're not using a password manager, choose three long words you'll remember.
prompt.changeEmail: $?Want to change your email? Submit a new one and we'll send a confirmation to the old one.
prompt.changePassword: $?Want to change your password? Submit a new one and repeat it.
prompt.email: $?Do you have an email-only account? Login with your email, and if there is account associated with it, we'll send you an email with sign-in link. New email-only accounts are not supported, to preserve privacy.
prompt.sent: $?If this email is associated with an account, it will receive an email with a login link. It may take a few minutes to appear. If there is no account associated with this email, you will not receive anything.
prompt.logout: $?Are you using a shared device and want to keep your projects private? Logout and we'll *delete your projects* from this device, but they will be saved online.
prompt.confirm: $?Check your old email address to confirm your new address.
prompt.delete: $?Want us to forget everything you've made here? This can't be undone.
prompt.reallyDelete: $?Are you sure? Your account and settings will be deleted immediately and your projects will be scheduled for deletion. Collaborators on your projects will immediately lose access to them. Type your email or username to confirm that this is what you want.
prompt.name: $?Pick an emoji or custom character to represent yourself to others.

error.invalidUsername: $?Usernames must be at least 5 letters with no spaces.
error.deletePartial: $?We removed your account's data, but couldn't finish removing your account. Please try again, or contact us if it keeps happening.
error.invalidPassword: $?Passwords must be at least 6 characters long.
error.wrongPassword: $?Not a valid username and password. Either your password is wrong, or someone else has this username.
error.tooMany: $?You've tried and failed to log in too many times. You'll have to wait to try again.
error.unsaved: $?You have changes that aren't saved online yet. Reconnect and wait for them to save first, or they'll be lost from this device.

username.description: $?login username, don't use personally identifiable information

button.login: $?create an account with this username and password

prompt.create: $?Create an account to save your projects, collaborate with others, and share your work.
prompt.username: $?Usernames must be at least /5 characters/, can't be an email, and shouldn't contain your name or other identifying information.
prompt.password: $?Passwords must be at least /6 characters/. Type it twice and write it down somewhere secure, like a password manager. *There is no way to recover your account* if you lose it, since we do not gather contact information.

content: $?Hi!

$?Let's set some expectations about your rights and ours (also in light of policy, such as the <COPPA@https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-16/chapter-I/subchapter-C/part-312> and <GDPR@https://gdpr.eu/compliance-checklist-us-companies/>).

$?The first thing to know is that we are not a commercial entity. We are a community-based research project housed at a not-for-profit university. Our goal is to create a platform that brings you joy and helps us make discoveries about a more equitable and just world of computing. We have no interest in making money on this platform; any money we gather (usually through public funding) is used to sustain the platform, not to enrich anyone who works on it (or contributes to it).

$?Because we are not seeking profit, this also means that we can't make any promises about the reliability, availability, or longevity of this platform. That said, <Amy@https://amyjko.phd> is committed long term to sustaining it, and as a tenured professor, she's got a pretty stable gig.
$?That brings us to *data*. Here's what we gather and store in the cloud:

$?• Your *projects*. We store any projects you contribute and your changes to them, unless those projects appear to have personally identifiable information such as phone numbers, email addresses, usernames, tax identifiers, or addresses.
$?• Your *settings*. This includes the languages you choose, your animation preferences, and your tutorial progress. Everything else is stored on your device only.

$?• Aggregate *activity*. We track logins and the pages you visit, but not in a way that can identify you, track you across the site, or track you across other sites. We use Google Analytics in 'consent denied' mode, which only gathers minimal non-identifiable information about page views, without storing cookies, or sending IP address information to Google. We use this aggregate information to help raise funding by reporting how much the platform is being used.
$?We don't store anything else. Our <source@https://github.com/amyjko/wordplay/tree/main/src> is public, so anyone can verify this, and report any unintended tracking.
$?*You* own your data, not us. That means:
$?• You control who can access your projects. They are private by default, but you can share them with individuals, groups, or make them entirely public.
$?• You can fully delete any project or your own account at any time.
$?• You can get an export of any project or your whole account at any time.
$?Here's how we will and won't use your data:

$?• We won't share your data with anyone, unless explicitly required by law. It's always possible that your data is taken without permission (a 'data breach'). If we discover that this has happened, we will notify you that this has happened using the email you've shared with us.
$?• We won't contact you via your email address unless you 1) make an account change that requires us to, 2) you explicitly give us consent to reach out, or 3) in the case of data breach above.

$?• We may analyze projects, galleries, and how to content on the platform to understand what everyone is making and how they are making it. We may share these aggregated, anonymized insights in academic publications. We will only do this under the supervision of an institutional review board, as mandated by U.S. federal law.

$?Finally, a note about speech. You can say anything you want on this platform in *private*. Projects are private by default, and if you share them with specific email addresses, they are still considered private. We won't moderate anything in private projects.

$?But making a project *public*, or including it in a public gallery, is a privilege. This is a platform made for love, affirmation, respect, and dignity. Therefore, we expect that none of your *public* content will:
consequences: $?Chats for projects and how-tos that are in public or private galleries are subject to moderation by the gallery curators. You can report messages that violate these community guidelines to the curators using the report button next to each message, and curators can approve or remove messages.

$?If we find a project or gallery that violates these rules, we will warn creators before they view it, or block it from being viewed. If you repeatedly violate these rules, you'll lose the privilege of posting publicly.

content: $?Wordplay is a free, community-based project supported by the <University of Washington@https://washington.edu>. We rely on gifts from people who believe in our mission of accessible, language-inclusive, educational programming languages.
$?Here are our current costs:

$?• We compensate University of Washington undergraduates, especially those who are disabled or whose first language was not English, to evolve and maintain the project. This is about 90% of our costs.
$?• We give stipends to teachers who we collaborate with to develop multilingual, accessible curricula.
$?• We pay Google for <Firebase@https://firebase.google.com/> bandwidth and storage, and <Workspace@https://workspace.google.com/> services.
$?• We pay <Squarespace@https://www.squarespace.com/> annually for the domain.

$?Our current costs, assuming 5 undergraduates at $$20 USD//hour, 10 hours//week during the academic year (36 weeks) and 2 undergraduates during the summer (12 weeks), plus cloud services, are about $$60K USD per year.
$?If 2,400 people gave $$25 USD per year, that would cover our current costs, with any excess going to compensate more students and teachers.
$?Can you be one of those 2,400 people? If so, here's our University of Washington giving link:

design.description: $?This is a reference for Wordplay's visual design system, including colors, fonts, spacing, and reusable components. See the <design wiki@https://github.com/wordplaydev/wordplay/wiki/design> and <develop wiki@https://github.com/wordplaydev/wordplay/wiki/develop> guides for contributing.

thanks.intro:
$?Thank you to our *$count* contributors for contributing to Wordplay's design, development, localization, and content! And thank you to the many teachers and youth who've guided our design in focus groups, design sessions, and classrooms, who we don't list here to maintain their privacy.

This project has been supported by the National Science Foundation under awards 2318257, 2031265, 2100296, and 2122950, the University of Washington, and generous <gifts@://donate> from people like you.
